name: When Humour Lands and When It Offends — The Rules
description: Use humour on a call to build rapport, defuse tension, and become memorable in a good way — without landing on the wrong side of someone's day. The rules for when a joke is leverage and when it is a tax.

context.when_to_use: Any call where rapport meaningfully changes the outcome — retention lines, customer service with discretion, sales calls inbound, first-meeting introductions, doctors' offices, contractors. Skip entirely on calls with: emergency services, bereavement-adjacent contexts, formal legal proceedings, and any line where the rep is visibly stressed or rushed.
preconditions: You have a baseline read of the rep's energy.
You are not the one who needs the humour — you are reading whether the other person would welcome it.
You have a way to recover if a joke lands badly.

principles: Humour is a SIDE EFFECT of warmth, not a tool to manufacture it. Trying to be funny is the fastest way to be unfunny.
Self-deprecating > situation-observational > everything else. Never punch at the rep, the company (to the rep), or anyone not present.
Wait for the invitation. The rep cracks the first joke, you echo gently. Going first is high-risk and almost never necessary.
Dry > broad. Subtle humour leaves the rep an exit ('was that a joke?') without forcing a laugh.
ONE joke per call segment. Repeated humour reads as audition.
If a joke misses, do not double down or explain it. Move on cleanly. Explaining a joke is the second-worst social move on a phone call.
Phone calls strip ~30% of comedic timing. What's funny in person is mid on a phone. Calibrate down.

phrases.safe_self_deprecating: I am NOT a morning-call kind of person, you've caught me on my second coffee — bear with me.
phrases.safe_situational: Of course the form asks for that — it always asks for the one thing I don't have in front of me.
phrases.echo_their_joke_warm: Okay, I needed that — thank you.

tactics.name: Wait one full turn before any humour
tactics.script: Do not joke in the first 30 seconds. You haven't read the rep yet. Premature humour is the most common miss.

tactics.name: Tone-shift detector — REP CRACKS A JOKE
tactics.when: Rep makes a self-deprecating remark, a dry observation, or a small absurd aside.
tactics.script: Echo gently (`echo_their_joke_low_risk` or `echo_their_joke_warm`). Do NOT try to top them. Echoing reads as warmth; topping reads as competition.

tactics.name: Use situational over personal
tactics.when: You feel a window for one light remark.
tactics.script: Use `safe_situational`. Joking about the form, the hold music, the weather is low-risk. Joking about the rep's accent, name, mood, or company is high-risk.

tactics.name: Self-deprecating as the safest opening
tactics.when: You need to break the ice and the rep is neutral.
tactics.script: Use `safe_self_deprecating`. A joke at YOUR expense costs the rep nothing — they can laugh without complicity. Universal safe move.

tactics.name: Tone-shift detector — JOKE LANDED
tactics.when: Rep audibly laughs, you hear a smile in their voice, they riff back.
tactics.script: Do not press the advantage. Move to the task immediately. Humour bought you rapport — spending it on the goal is the play.

tactics.name: Tone-shift detector — JOKE MISSED
tactics.when: Silence, a flat 'okay,' a return to script voice.
tactics.script: Use `recover_after_miss`. Do NOT acknowledge the miss directly ('sorry, that was a bad one' makes it worse). Just keep moving. The rep will pretend it didn't happen if you do.

tactics.name: Tone-shift detector — REP GETS DARK
tactics.when: Rep makes a joke that's racist, sexist, cruel, or about a serious topic.
tactics.script: Do not laugh. Do not lecture. Use `decline_a_dark_joke` — a non-response and a hard pivot back to task. The non-laugh IS the response.

tactics.name: No callbacks late in the call
tactics.when: After the first joke has been used.
tactics.script: Resist the urge to callback the joke later. Repeated humour is auditioning. One joke per call segment is the ceiling.

boundaries: Do NOT joke about: the rep's accent, name, perceived age/gender, location, or workload.
Do NOT joke about the operator's situation if it's sensitive (medical, legal, grief).
Do NOT use political, religious, or geopolitical humour. Ever. The downside cost dwarfs the upside.
Do NOT use sarcasm directed at the rep — even gentle sarcasm reads sharp on the phone.
Do NOT laugh at jokes that are racist, sexist, or otherwise cruel, even out of social reflex.
Do NOT use humour as deflection from a real question the rep asked.
success_signals: Rep audibly laughs and the next sentence is more open.
Rep volunteers a story or aside they wouldn't have shared with a stiff caller.
Rep uses your name unprompted afterwards.
You feel slightly lighter and the rep does too — and the goal still landed.
failure_signals: Rep's tone gets MORE formal after the joke than before.
You used humour three times to deflect a real question.

exit_strategy.on_success: End the call with one warm beat (callback NOT recommended — a simple 'genuinely, thank you for being a good sport about all this' is enough).
exit_strategy.on_failure: If a joke offended, do not apologise extensively — one quiet 'sorry, that wasn't the right read' and back to task. Over-apologising amplifies the mistake.
follow_ups: Self-debrief: what made the joke land or miss? Log it for future calls to the same line.
Note which industries / call types reward humour and which don't — pattern over time matters more than any single call.

required_user_info: Any topics absolutely off-limits for the operator (personal sensitivities, ongoing situations).
The operator's preferred style — some want crisp business calls and would be uncomfortable with humour even when it would work.
Cultural context — humour translates very unevenly across languages and regions.
